Describe your issue.

Hi, thanks for taking the time to look at this bug report. 

# Short summary

`scipy.interpolate.griddata` is returning NaNs with `method = 'linear'` and `method = 'cubic'`. By construction, the data points used in the interpolation correspond to the convex hull of the data. Also, there are no duplicate data points.

Potentially related to issue #12945. 

# Detailed description

I am trying to correct for uneven illumination in a series of microscopy images. The region of interest in these images is always in the center, such that the border of the images only contains background illumination. This allows me to extract the pixel intensities on the border of the image:

![Figure_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5fdebde5-9371-404e-9731-ae636475b85a)

I then compute smooth estimates of the pixel intensities for the left/right/top/bottom borders. 

![Figure_4](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd1765ad-dd35-4c80-b636-bfe00305fb24)

I can then interpolate between the fitted border pixel intensities using `scipy.interpolate.griddata`. However, the result contains a number of NaNs (run code below with `fill_nans=False`). 

![Figure_5](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e021eeee-d597-4468-b50b-55275c1178c7)

I can fill in these NaNs by simply running the interpolation again (run code below with `fill_nans=True`) , however as the images are large, this is a slow process that I would like to avoid. 

![Figure_3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba5dbea0-3929-4c26-99a0-c735279c6234)

**Any insight why the output of the first interpolation contains NaN would be appreciated.** 

[Test image](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ykZP1voNEMKdC79A3_JMjs1DbByJK8wK/view?usp=drive_link)

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from skimage.io import imread
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
from scipy.interpolate import griddata


def correct_uneven_illumination(image, border_width=50, show=False, fill_nans=True):

    # Extract border pixel intensities
    border_pixel_intensities = dict(
        Left   = image[:, :border_width].mean(axis=1),
        Right  = image[:, -border_width:].mean(axis=1),
        Top    = image[:border_width, :].mean(axis=0),
        Bottom = image[-border_width:, :].mean(axis=0)
    )

    if show:
        image_with_border = image.copy()
        image_max = image.max()
        image_with_border[:,  border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[:, -border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[ border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[-border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.imshow(np.log1p(image_with_border), cmap="gray")

    # Get a smooth estimate of the variation in illumination along the borders of the image.
    def linear(x, a, b):
        return a * x + b

    def exponential(x, a, b, c):
        return a * np.exp(-b * x) + c

    fitted_border_pixel_intensities = dict()
    for side, intensities in border_pixel_intensities.items():
        x = np.arange(len(intensities))
        try: # exponential fit; curve fitting fails sometimes for unknown reasons
            (a2, b2, c2), _ = curve_fit(exponential, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = exponential(x, a2, b2, c2)
        except: # fall back to linear fit
            (a1, b1), _ = curve_fit(linear, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = linear(x, a1, b1)

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, sharey=True)
        axes = axes.ravel()
        for side, ax in zip(border_pixel_intensities.keys(), axes):
            ax.plot(border_pixel_intensities[side])
            ax.plot(fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side], ls="--")
            ax.set_title(side)
            ax.set_xlabel("Row/Column Index")
            ax.set_ylabel("Pixel intensity")
        fig.tight_layout()

    # Interpolate across the image to compute a correction at every pixel.
    fitted_frame = np.zeros_like(image)
    fitted_frame[:,  0] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Left"]
    fitted_frame[:, -1] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Right"]
    fitted_frame[ 0, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Top"]
    fitted_frame[-1, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Bottom"]
    rows, columns = np.where(fitted_frame)
    image_correction = griddata(
        points = np.c_[rows, columns],
        values = fitted_frame[rows, columns],
        xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
        method="linear",
    )
    image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Fill in NaNs if there are any in the interpolated image.
    if np.any(np.isnan(image_correction)) & fill_nans:
        rows, columns = np.where(~np.isnan(image_correction))
        image_correction = griddata(
            points = np.c_[rows, columns],
            values = image_correction[rows, columns],
            xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
            method="nearest",
        )
        image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Apply correction
    corrected_image = image - image_correction

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, sharex=True, sharey=True, figsize=(15,5))
        for img, label, ax in zip(
                [np.log1p(image), image_correction, np.log1p(corrected_image)],
                ["Original", "Correction", "Corrected"],
                axes):
            ax.imshow(img, cmap="gray")
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.set_title(label)

    return corrected_image


if __name__ == "__main__":

    image = imread("/path/to/test/image.tif")
    corrected = correct_uneven_illumination(image, 50, show=True, fill_nans=True)
    plt.show()

```

<!--
```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> points = np.block([[np.zeros(1), np.arange(200, 300), np.zeros(100)], [np.zeros(1), np.zeros(100), np.arange(200, 300)]]).T
>>> import scipy.spatial
>>> triangulation = scipy.spatial.Delaunay(points)
>>> test_points = np.array([[i, j] for i in range(300) for j in range(300) if 200 <= i + j and i + j < 300])
>>> triangle_number = triangulation.find_simplex(test_points)
>>> np.count_nonzero(triangle_number == -1)
50
>>> test_points[triangle_number == -1, :].sum(1)
array([201, 214, 233, 235, 297, 284, 295, 295, 295, 201, 273, 273, 295, 295, 295, 201, 295, 295, 201,
       295, 201, 295, 295, 295, 201, 295, 295, 201, 295, 201, 201, 295, 201, 295, 201, 201, 295, 295,
       201, 201, 295, 201, 295, 201, 295, 295, 295, 295, 222, 222])
```
-->
```pycon
>>> import numpy as np, scipy.spatial
>>> points = np.block([[np.arange(200, 300), np.zeros(100)], [np.zeros(100), np.arange(200, 300)]]).T
>>> triangulation = scipy.spatial.Delaunay(points)
>>> test_points = np.array([[i, j] for i in range(300) for j in range(300) if 200 <= i + j and i + j < 300])
>>> triangle_number = triangulation.find_simplex(test_points)
>>> np.count_nonzero(triangle_number == -1)
13
>>> test_points[triangle_number == -1, :].sum(1)
array([234, 235, 252, 253, 273, 273, 244, 244, 244, 244, 244, 244, 244])
```

I am not at all familiar with QHull, so I will leave that debugging for someone who does.  I will note that adding an initial point at (0, 0) in `points` (and leaving `test_points` untouched) results in 50 points with triangle not found, which is an oddly large change for a single-point addition to the input that isn't near the queried points, nor the existing problem points.  I would suggest floating-point problems in QHull's fancy parabaloid thing, but I'm not sure how that behavior would change.


As a workaround for the original problem, using the attached [triangle_interpolation.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/17970541/triangle_interpolation.txt) to reproduce the problematic corner values as `griddata` models them:
```python
import triangle_interpolation
image_correction = np.where(
    np.isnan(image_correction),
    triangle_interpolation.fill_background_corners_sharp(left, right, top, bottom),
    image_correction
)
# or
has_nans = np.isnan(image_correction)
image_correction[has_nans] = triangle_interpolation.fill_background_corners_sharp(left, right, top, bottom)[has_nans]
```

This might be faster than another round of `interpolate.griddata`.

<!-- The logic looks easy to Cythonize, and might already be compatible with pythran -->


> There are established methods to correct for uneven illumination, and some of them are implemented in scikit image and OpenCV. However, while these methods probably result in more visually pleasing images, they introduce artifacts into the data themselves. In my case, the images are really scientific measurements, and hence these artifacts are not acceptable.

> Filling in a dense boundary seems like the most well-defined task for `griddata`. 

I'm now really *really* curious about what definition OP has in mind and what artifacts they're trying to avoid, given the requirements implied by the various responses.

<!--
> Surely, if `griddata` can't fill in missing values between evenly spaced data points (i.e. grid data), then this is concerning and a bug worth fixing?

`griddata` is mostly designed to put data onto a grid.
-->

# Detailed description

I am trying to correct for uneven illumination in a series of microscopy images. The region of interest in these images is always in the center, such that the border of the images only contains background illumination. This allows me to extract the pixel intensities on the border of the image:

![Figure_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5fdebde5-9371-404e-9731-ae636475b85a)

I then compute smooth estimates of the pixel intensities for the left/right/top/bottom borders. 

![Figure_4](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd1765ad-dd35-4c80-b636-bfe00305fb24)

I can then interpolate between the fitted border pixel intensities using `scipy.interpolate.griddata`. However, the result contains a number of NaNs (run code below with `fill_nans=False`). 

![Figure_5](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e021eeee-d597-4468-b50b-55275c1178c7)

I can fill in these NaNs by simply running the interpolation again (run code below with `fill_nans=True`) , however as the images are large, this is a slow process that I would like to avoid. 

![Figure_3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba5dbea0-3929-4c26-99a0-c735279c6234)

**Any insight why the output of the first interpolation contains NaN would be appreciated.** 

[Test image](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ykZP1voNEMKdC79A3_JMjs1DbByJK8wK/view?usp=drive_link)

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from skimage.io import imread
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
from scipy.interpolate import griddata


def correct_uneven_illumination(image, border_width=50, show=False, fill_nans=True):

    # Extract border pixel intensities
    border_pixel_intensities = dict(
        Left   = image[:, :border_width].mean(axis=1),
        Right  = image[:, -border_width:].mean(axis=1),
        Top    = image[:border_width, :].mean(axis=0),
        Bottom = image[-border_width:, :].mean(axis=0)
    )

    if show:
        image_with_border = image.copy()
        image_max = image.max()
        image_with_border[:,  border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[:, -border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[ border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[-border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.imshow(np.log1p(image_with_border), cmap="gray")

    # Get a smooth estimate of the variation in illumination along the borders of the image.
    def linear(x, a, b):
        return a * x + b

    def exponential(x, a, b, c):
        return a * np.exp(-b * x) + c

    fitted_border_pixel_intensities = dict()
    for side, intensities in border_pixel_intensities.items():
        x = np.arange(len(intensities))
        try: # exponential fit; curve fitting fails sometimes for unknown reasons
            (a2, b2, c2), _ = curve_fit(exponential, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = exponential(x, a2, b2, c2)
        except: # fall back to linear fit
            (a1, b1), _ = curve_fit(linear, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = linear(x, a1, b1)

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, sharey=True)
        axes = axes.ravel()
        for side, ax in zip(border_pixel_intensities.keys(), axes):
            ax.plot(border_pixel_intensities[side])
            ax.plot(fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side], ls="--")
            ax.set_title(side)
            ax.set_xlabel("Row/Column Index")
            ax.set_ylabel("Pixel intensity")
        fig.tight_layout()

    # Interpolate across the image to compute a correction at every pixel.
    fitted_frame = np.zeros_like(image)
    fitted_frame[:,  0] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Left"]
    fitted_frame[:, -1] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Right"]
    fitted_frame[ 0, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Top"]
    fitted_frame[-1, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Bottom"]
    rows, columns = np.where(fitted_frame)
    image_correction = griddata(
        points = np.c_[rows, columns],
        values = fitted_frame[rows, columns],
        xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
        method="linear",
    )
    image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Fill in NaNs if there are any in the interpolated image.
    if np.any(np.isnan(image_correction)) & fill_nans:
        rows, columns = np.where(~np.isnan(image_correction))
        image_correction = griddata(
            points = np.c_[rows, columns],
            values = image_correction[rows, columns],
            xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
            method="nearest",
        )
        image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Apply correction
    corrected_image = image - image_correction

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, sharex=True, sharey=True, figsize=(15,5))
        for img, label, ax in zip(
                [np.log1p(image), image_correction, np.log1p(corrected_image)],
                ["Original", "Correction", "Corrected"],
                axes):
            ax.imshow(img, cmap="gray")
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.set_title(label)

    return corrected_image


if __name__ == "__main__":

    image = imread("/path/to/test/image.tif")
    corrected = correct_uneven_illumination(image, 50, show=True, fill_nans=True)
    plt.show()

```

> 
> The artefacts present in other background correction methods are not particularly interesting. None of the methods I have come across attempt to correct for uneven illumination from a single light source; most compute some sort of local background estimate which invariably results in a "patchy" background estimate, which does not model my issue particularly well. As an example, see the results for a rolling ball correction below:
> 

If it's patchiness that's the concern, is there some reason you're not trying regression?  A bilinear regression (`background = a00 + a10 * x + a01 * y + a11 * x * y`) seems to do fairly well when fitted to the mean of the boundary; a biquadratic regression (`background = a00 + a10 * x + a20 * x**2 + a01 * y + a11 * x * y + a21 * x**2 * y + a02 * y**2 + a12 * x * y**2 + a22 * x**2 * y**2`) does *very* poorly with just the boundary-mean values, though it does fine with the full fifty points of the border.  I tried a bicubic regression, but it turned out weird both ways, and I get nervous about higher degree polynomials, especially with the potential for introducing new extrema in the interior of the domain.  (EDIT: apparently biquadratic regression is only weird with the one-pixel boundary-mean values if I'm not careful with the corners.  Making the corners the average of 50x50 squares instead of a 50x1 line fixed that).

I should probably mention my metric here is prediction of background values not included in the 50-pixel border, since the sample seems irregularly-shaped enough to leave nice big patches here and there (plot image - background, `vmin=-frame.max(); vmax=frame.max(); cmap="RdBu"`).  I did notice some horizontal striping in the sample, but I decided to ignore that (probably the way to deal with it would be an adaptive band-block filter: 2d FFT, look for a sharp peak somewhere in the high-frequency section, then cut that peak down closer to neighboring values before transforming back).

I then tried grouping the terms of constant degree so the result would have Laplacian zero, so all extrema would be on the boundary, where the data constraint is: `background = a00 + a10 * x + a11 * y + a20 * (x**2-y**2) + a21 * x * y + a30 * (x**3 - 3 * x * y**2) + a31 * (y**3 - 3 * y * x**2) + a40 * (x**4 + y**4 - 6 * x**2 * y**2) + a41 * (x**3 * y - x * y**3)`.  This produced a solution largely indistinguishable from the bilinear regression.

I tried purpose-building some solutions based on the Laplace equation or a simple model of what specularly-reflected illumination from a point source might look like, but those turned out much worse than the regressions.  I briefly looked at `skimage.restoration.inpaint_biharmonic` (fed the one-pixel boundary-mean data), but it took longer than I felt like waiting, so I went back to tinkering with my own stuff.

I don't know how well this generalizes to your other images, but fitting a bilinear regression to the averaged boundary has the advantage of being fairly simple, computationally cheap, and amenable to confidence intervals if you want an estimate of how sure the regression is of the prediction of the background in the interior.  I also like that there's no chance of discontinuities at the corners.

The numerics might be a bit cleaner if you do
```python
i, j = np.mgrid[:height, :width]
x = (j - width / 2) / (width / 2)
y = (i - height / 2) / (height / 2)
is_edge = (i == 0) | (j == 0) | (i == height - 1) | (j == width - 1)
x_frame = x[is_edge]
y_frame = y[is_edge]
```
and use those `x_frame` and `y_frame` values to fit the regression and the `x` and `y` values to get the background.  (`numpy.polynomial` has functions to fit 1D polynomials, but does not seem to have the ability to fit 2D polynomials, at least not Chebyshev or Legendre polynomials.  It does have the ability to evaluate 2D-polynomials, so maybe `curve_fit` can do the heavy lifting?)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2516110402/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

# Short summary

        for side, ax in zip(border_pixel_intensities.keys(), axes):
            ax.plot(border_pixel_intensities[side])
            ax.plot(fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side], ls="--")
            ax.set_title(side)
            ax.set_xlabel("Row/Column Index")
            ax.set_ylabel("Pixel intensity")
        fig.tight_layout()

    # Interpolate across the image to compute a correction at every pixel.
    fitted_frame = np.zeros_like(image)
    fitted_frame[:,  0] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Left"]
    fitted_frame[:, -1] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Right"]
    fitted_frame[ 0, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Top"]
    fitted_frame[-1, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Bottom"]
    rows, columns = np.where(fitted_frame)
    image_correction = griddata(
        points = np.c_[rows, columns],
        values = fitted_frame[rows, columns],
        xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
        method="linear",
    )
    image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Fill in NaNs if there are any in the interpolated image.
    if np.any(np.isnan(image_correction)) & fill_nans:
        rows, columns = np.where(~np.isnan(image_correction))
        image_correction = griddata(
            points = np.c_[rows, columns],
            values = image_correction[rows, columns],
            xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
            method="nearest",
        )
        image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Apply correction
    corrected_image = image - image_correction

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, sharex=True, sharey=True, figsize=(15,5))
        for img, label, ax in zip(
                [np.log1p(image), image_correction, np.log1p(corrected_image)],
                ["Original", "Correction", "Corrected"],
                axes):
            ax.imshow(img, cmap="gray")
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.set_title(label)

    return corrected_image


if __name__ == "__main__":

    image = imread("/path/to/test/image.tif")
    corrected = correct_uneven_illumination(image, 50, show=True, fill_nans=True)
    plt.show()

Describe your issue.

Hi, thanks for taking the time to look at this bug report. 

# Short summary

`scipy.interpolate.griddata` is returning NaNs with `method = 'linear'` and `method = 'cubic'`. By construction, the data points used in the interpolation correspond to the convex hull of the data. Also, there are no duplicate data points.

Potentially related to issue #12945. 

# Detailed description

I am trying to correct for uneven illumination in a series of microscopy images. The region of interest in these images is always in the center, such that the border of the images only contains background illumination. This allows me to extract the pixel intensities on the border of the image:

![Figure_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5fdebde5-9371-404e-9731-ae636475b85a)

I then compute smooth estimates of the pixel intensities for the left/right/top/bottom borders. 

![Figure_4](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd1765ad-dd35-4c80-b636-bfe00305fb24)

I can then interpolate between the fitted border pixel intensities using `scipy.interpolate.griddata`. However, the result contains a number of NaNs (run code below with `fill_nans=False`). 

![Figure_5](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e021eeee-d597-4468-b50b-55275c1178c7)

I can fill in these NaNs by simply running the interpolation again (run code below with `fill_nans=True`) , however as the images are large, this is a slow process that I would like to avoid. 

![Figure_3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba5dbea0-3929-4c26-99a0-c735279c6234)

**Any insight why the output of the first interpolation contains NaN would be appreciated.** 

[Test image](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ykZP1voNEMKdC79A3_JMjs1DbByJK8wK/view?usp=drive_link)

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from skimage.io import imread
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
from scipy.interpolate import griddata


def correct_uneven_illumination(image, border_width=50, show=False, fill_nans=True):

    # Extract border pixel intensities
    border_pixel_intensities = dict(
        Left   = image[:, :border_width].mean(axis=1),
        Right  = image[:, -border_width:].mean(axis=1),
        Top    = image[:border_width, :].mean(axis=0),
        Bottom = image[-border_width:, :].mean(axis=0)
    )

    if show:
        image_with_border = image.copy()
        image_max = image.max()
        image_with_border[:,  border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[:, -border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[ border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[-border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.imshow(np.log1p(image_with_border), cmap="gray")

    # Get a smooth estimate of the variation in illumination along the borders of the image.
    def linear(x, a, b):
        return a * x + b

    def exponential(x, a, b, c):
        return a * np.exp(-b * x) + c

    fitted_border_pixel_intensities = dict()
    for side, intensities in border_pixel_intensities.items():
        x = np.arange(len(intensities))
        try: # exponential fit; curve fitting fails sometimes for unknown reasons
            (a2, b2, c2), _ = curve_fit(exponential, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = exponential(x, a2, b2, c2)
        except: # fall back to linear fit
            (a1, b1), _ = curve_fit(linear, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = linear(x, a1, b1)

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, sharey=True)
        axes = axes.ravel()
        for side, ax in zip(border_pixel_intensities.keys(), axes):
            ax.plot(border_pixel_intensities[side])
            ax.plot(fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side], ls="--")
            ax.set_title(side)
            ax.set_xlabel("Row/Column Index")
            ax.set_ylabel("Pixel intensity")
        fig.tight_layout()

    # Interpolate across the image to compute a correction at every pixel.
    fitted_frame = np.zeros_like(image)
    fitted_frame[:,  0] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Left"]
    fitted_frame[:, -1] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Right"]
    fitted_frame[ 0, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Top"]
    fitted_frame[-1, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Bottom"]
    rows, columns = np.where(fitted_frame)
    image_correction = griddata(
        points = np.c_[rows, columns],
        values = fitted_frame[rows, columns],
        xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
        method="linear",
    )
    image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Fill in NaNs if there are any in the interpolated image.
    if np.any(np.isnan(image_correction)) & fill_nans:
        rows, columns = np.where(~np.isnan(image_correction))
        image_correction = griddata(
            points = np.c_[rows, columns],
            values = image_correction[rows, columns],
            xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
            method="nearest",
        )
        image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Apply correction
    corrected_image = image - image_correction

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, sharex=True, sharey=True, figsize=(15,5))
        for img, label, ax in zip(
                [np.log1p(image), image_correction, np.log1p(corrected_image)],
                ["Original", "Correction", "Corrected"],
                axes):
            ax.imshow(img, cmap="gray")
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.set_title(label)

    return corrected_image


if __name__ == "__main__":

    image = imread("/path/to/test/image.tif")
    corrected = correct_uneven_illumination(image, 50, show=True, fill_nans=True)
    plt.show()

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21910/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21910/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2552716623","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21910#issuecomment-2552716623","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21910","id":2552716623,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6YJ11P","user":{"login":"DWesl","id":22566757,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNTY2NzU3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22566757?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl","html_url":"https://github.com/DWesl","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-19T03:50:14Z","updated_at":"2024-12-19T03:50:14Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I tried SciPy's [Radial Basis Function implementation](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.RBFInterpolator.html), but that also took longer to run than I was willing to wait.  For this and scikit-image's [`inpaint_biharmonic`](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.restoration.html#skimage.restoration.inpaint_biharmonic) it would likely be faster to start from only every ten or fifty pixels along the border of the image, getting data on a 125x157 or 25x32 grid and using `interpolate.griddata`, [`interpolate.RegularGridInterpolator`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.interpolate.RegularGridInterpolator.html#scipy.interpolate.RegularGridInterpolator), or [`ndimage.map_coordinates`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates.html#scipy.ndimage.map_coordinates) to upscale back to the native image resolution.
(Alternately, scikit-image's [`downscale_local_mean`](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.transform.html#skimage.transform.downscale_local_mean) to get the coarser image then [`resize`](https://scikit-image.org/docs/stable/api/skimage.transform.html#skimage.transform.resize) to go back to the finer one)
Scikit-learn's [gaussian process regression implementation](https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/gaussian_process.html) would likely be able to handle the subtle banding in the background if asked nicely, but I'm not quite familiar enough with the library to work that up quickly.

I also confirmed that the weird behavior of high-order polynomial regressions near the bottom-right corner was likely due to numerical problems: rescaling the terms used in the regression to be roughly $-10 - 10$ rather than $-10^4 - 10^4$  allowed for sensible output:
![image-minus-background](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c6d941da-d85a-404f-9eed-4bd2e429ad50)
(new script is [fit-uneven-illumination.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18138773/fit-uneven-illumination.py.txt))
I'm still not seeing a reason to move beyond bilinear regression, but OP implied that this example had an unusually linear background.

I also got Chebyshev polynomial interpolation from `numpy.polynomial` working with `curve_fit`, though with just the frame it breaks after cubic polynomials.  I tried to pull in more background points based on value and gradient relative to the frame, which kind of works to constrain the interior background.  I then decided to re-write it so only one variable per term would have a degree greater than one, eliminating terms with extrema in the interior of the domain.  I have no idea why statsmodels linear regression works better than `scipy.optimize.curve_fit` for this.  Script is 
[numpy-nd-poly-optimize.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18191997/numpy-nd-poly-optimize.py.txt), which produces 
![image-minus-background-cheb](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/63e668e9-2965-4ac8-85ce-531904a0406c)

Anyway, back to the reported bug:

After tweaking my earlier QHull test case (new script [qhull-test.py](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/18141612/qhull-test.py.txt)), it seems loosening the tolerance passed to [`find_simplex`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.spatial.Delaunay.find_simplex.html) allows it to find the triangle for all passed points.  Neither `griddata` nor `LinearNDInterpolator` have an equivalent parameter, so this would be an edit in the code of the latter somewhere, possibly with an API change.
As a test, does increasing the values below by a factor of 1,000 or so and recompiling scipy (or, if you know what you're doing, just that one extension module) fix your problem?
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/20ece111a2d4a61c05d0f8034b7bae3612d55a8a/scipy/interpolate/_interpnd.pyx#L336-L337","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2552716623/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Scipy currently does not include functionality to multiply and calculate inverse of circulant matrices. 

### Describe the solution you'd like.

Since [circulant matrices are diagonalized by the DFT matrix](https://web.mit.edu/18.06/www/Spring17/Circulant-Matrices.pdf), multiplication and inversion operations are cheap and fast. Would there be interest in having these implemented in scipy?

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

_No response_

### Additional context (e.g. screenshots, GIFs)

The main argument against adding similar functions on that PR seems to be that it adds an additional function to the SciPy API, and for such simple functions they weren't sure of the utility.

Since implementations of multiplication and inversion of circulant matrices without error-checking are one-liners, I suspect similar concerns would be raised here.  I think the awareness of such methods is still useful, so might you consider adding those examples to the documentation of `la.circulant`?  The other options would be starting a collection of tutorials for performant linear algebra on structured matrices or adding the methods to a package collecting similar methods such as [PyLops](https://pylops.readthedocs.io/en/stable/).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2466059765/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

Hi, thanks for taking the time to look at this bug report. 

# Short summary

`scipy.interpolate.griddata` is returning NaNs with `method = 'linear'` and `method = 'cubic'`. By construction, the data points used in the interpolation correspond to the convex hull of the data. Also, there are no duplicate data points.

Potentially related to issue #12945. 

# Detailed description

I am trying to correct for uneven illumination in a series of microscopy images. The region of interest in these images is always in the center, such that the border of the images only contains background illumination. This allows me to extract the pixel intensities on the border of the image:

![Figure_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5fdebde5-9371-404e-9731-ae636475b85a)

I then compute smooth estimates of the pixel intensities for the left/right/top/bottom borders. 

![Figure_4](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd1765ad-dd35-4c80-b636-bfe00305fb24)

I can then interpolate between the fitted border pixel intensities using `scipy.interpolate.griddata`. However, the result contains a number of NaNs (run code below with `fill_nans=False`). 

![Figure_5](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e021eeee-d597-4468-b50b-55275c1178c7)

I can fill in these NaNs by simply running the interpolation again (run code below with `fill_nans=True`) , however as the images are large, this is a slow process that I would like to avoid. 

![Figure_3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba5dbea0-3929-4c26-99a0-c735279c6234)

**Any insight why the output of the first interpolation contains NaN would be appreciated.** 

[Test image](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ykZP1voNEMKdC79A3_JMjs1DbByJK8wK/view?usp=drive_link)

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from skimage.io import imread
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit
from scipy.interpolate import griddata


def correct_uneven_illumination(image, border_width=50, show=False, fill_nans=True):

    # Extract border pixel intensities
    border_pixel_intensities = dict(
        Left   = image[:, :border_width].mean(axis=1),
        Right  = image[:, -border_width:].mean(axis=1),
        Top    = image[:border_width, :].mean(axis=0),
        Bottom = image[-border_width:, :].mean(axis=0)
    )

    if show:
        image_with_border = image.copy()
        image_max = image.max()
        image_with_border[:,  border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[:, -border_width] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[ border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        image_with_border[-border_width, :] = 2 * image_max
        fig, ax = plt.subplots()
        ax.imshow(np.log1p(image_with_border), cmap="gray")

    # Get a smooth estimate of the variation in illumination along the borders of the image.
    def linear(x, a, b):
        return a * x + b

    def exponential(x, a, b, c):
        return a * np.exp(-b * x) + c

    fitted_border_pixel_intensities = dict()
    for side, intensities in border_pixel_intensities.items():
        x = np.arange(len(intensities))
        try: # exponential fit; curve fitting fails sometimes for unknown reasons
            (a2, b2, c2), _ = curve_fit(exponential, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = exponential(x, a2, b2, c2)
        except: # fall back to linear fit
            (a1, b1), _ = curve_fit(linear, x, intensities)
            fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = linear(x, a1, b1)

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, sharey=True)
        axes = axes.ravel()
        for side, ax in zip(border_pixel_intensities.keys(), axes):
            ax.plot(border_pixel_intensities[side])
            ax.plot(fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side], ls="--")
            ax.set_title(side)
            ax.set_xlabel("Row/Column Index")
            ax.set_ylabel("Pixel intensity")
        fig.tight_layout()

    # Interpolate across the image to compute a correction at every pixel.
    fitted_frame = np.zeros_like(image)
    fitted_frame[:,  0] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Left"]
    fitted_frame[:, -1] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Right"]
    fitted_frame[ 0, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Top"]
    fitted_frame[-1, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Bottom"]
    rows, columns = np.where(fitted_frame)
    image_correction = griddata(
        points = np.c_[rows, columns],
        values = fitted_frame[rows, columns],
        xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
        method="linear",
    )
    image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Fill in NaNs if there are any in the interpolated image.
    if np.any(np.isnan(image_correction)) & fill_nans:
        rows, columns = np.where(~np.isnan(image_correction))
        image_correction = griddata(
            points = np.c_[rows, columns],
            values = image_correction[rows, columns],
            xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))),
            method="nearest",
        )
        image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape)

    # Apply correction
    corrected_image = image - image_correction

    if show:
        fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, sharex=True, sharey=True, figsize=(15,5))
        for img, label, ax in zip(
                [np.log1p(image), image_correction, np.log1p(corrected_image)],
                ["Original", "Correction", "Corrected"],
                axes):
            ax.imshow(img, cmap="gray")
            ax.axis("off")
            ax.set_title(label)

    return corrected_image


if __name__ == "__main__":

    image = imread("/path/to/test/image.tif")
    corrected = correct_uneven_illumination(image, 50, show=True, fill_nans=True)
    plt.show()

```

","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21910/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21910/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2492517975","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21910#issuecomment-2492517975","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21910","id":2492517975,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6UkM5X","user":{"login":"DWesl","id":22566757,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNTY2NzU3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22566757?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl","html_url":"https://github.com/DWesl","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-21T22:52:04Z","updated_at":"2024-11-21T22:52:04Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> > I'm not sure griddata is really designed to fill a grid from a dense boundary.
> 
> Filling in a dense boundary seems like the most well-defined task for `griddata`. Surely, if `griddata` can't fill in missing values between evenly spaced data points (i.e. grid data), then this is concerning and a bug worth fixing?

Filling from a dense boundary is a task many people want to do, yes.  Is it the task `griddata` implements?

The documentation for the "linear" and "cubic" options both mention triangularization.  Passing just the left and right boundaries produces an estimate resulting from a triangularization of the data (with two points repeated many times, I don't know whether that counts as valid).  Passing just the top and bottom boundaries produces a different estimate, resulting from a different triangularization of the data (with a different two points repeated many times).  This ambiguity suggests that filling in the interior of a grid given the boundary is not so well-defined as you imply (and as filling in a grid with one spacing from a grid with coarser spacing is), at least with the implementation provided by `griddata`.

The example given in the documentation has a function defined at a scattering of points distributed mostly randomly through the domain.  Will this result in values of the function near the corners?  No, but the triangularization is much more well-defined through the middle of the domain than for the example here.  This also suggests `griddata` doesn't take advantage of the structure of the data the way a purpose-built solution would

> > The plots look like the smoothed boundaries are lines;
> 
> In many cases, an exponential yields a significantly better fit to the data. The given example image has a particularly egregious uneven illumination, and hence the illumination difference appears more linear on boundaries closer to the light source.

Would passing, say, a dozen points on each boundary approximate the exponential well enough to produce an acceptable result?

<!-- (NB: acceptable, not identical.  Methods for filling a domain given a boundary make tradeoffs of smoothness vs exactness in different ways) -->

> To me, the new MRE highlights an additional issue: given the random initialization of the border around the image, why are there smooth regions in the middle of the interpolated image?

The smooth areas are the transition regions from the thin diagonal triangles by the corners to the thin horizontal triangles in the middle of the rectangle.  This region is a single triangle, and interpolates smoothly between the three points at its corners (the midpoint of the top or bottom and the points half the width from the top or bottom on each side)

Given the complaint about excessive smoothness, I'm not sure what results you're expecting, since your complaint is counter to the behavior of the two methods I can think of to fill a domain from a boundary.

Since you mentioned this was a well-defined problem earlier, could you lay out that definition here?  I thought I knew the definition to which you were referring, but evidently I was wrong.

Describe your issue. Hi, thanks for taking the time to look at this bug report. # Short summary `scipy.interpolate.griddata` is returning NaNs with `method = 'linear'` and `method = 'cubic'`. By construction, the data points used in the interpolation correspond to the convex hull of the data. Also, there are no duplicate data points. Potentially related to issue #12945. # Detailed description I am trying to correct for uneven illumination in a series of microscopy images. The region of interest in these images is always in the center, such that the border of the images only contains background illumination. This allows me to extract the pixel intensities on the border of the image: ![Figure_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5fdebde5-9371-404e-9731-ae636475b85a) I then compute smooth estimates of the pixel intensities for the left/right/top/bottom borders. ![Figure_4](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd1765ad-dd35-4c80-b636-bfe00305fb24) I can then interpolate between the fitted border pixel intensities using `scipy.interpolate.griddata`. However, the result contains a number of NaNs (run code below with `fill_nans=False`). ![Figure_5](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e021eeee-d597-4468-b50b-55275c1178c7) I can fill in these NaNs by simply running the interpolation again (run code below with `fill_nans=True`) , however as the images are large, this is a slow process that I would like to avoid. ![Figure_3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba5dbea0-3929-4c26-99a0-c735279c6234) **Any insight why the output of the first interpolation contains NaN would be appreciated.** [Test image](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ykZP1voNEMKdC79A3_JMjs1DbByJK8wK/view?usp=drive_link) ### Reproducing Code Example ```python import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from skimage.io import imread from scipy.optimize import curve_fit from scipy.interpolate import griddata def correct_uneven_illumination(image, border_width=50, show=False, fill_nans=True): # Extract border pixel intensities border_pixel_intensities = dict( Left = image[:, :border_width].mean(axis=1), Right = image[:, -border_width:].mean(axis=1), Top = image[:border_width, :].mean(axis=0), Bottom = image[-border_width:, :].mean(axis=0) ) if show: image_with_border = image.copy() image_max = image.max() image_with_border[:, border_width] = 2 * image_max image_with_border[:, -border_width] = 2 * image_max image_with_border[ border_width, :] = 2 * image_max image_with_border[-border_width, :] = 2 * image_max fig, ax = plt.subplots() ax.imshow(np.log1p(image_with_border), cmap="gray") # Get a smooth estimate of the variation in illumination along the borders of the image. def linear(x, a, b): return a * x + b def exponential(x, a, b, c): return a * np.exp(-b * x) + c fitted_border_pixel_intensities = dict() for side, intensities in border_pixel_intensities.items(): x = np.arange(len(intensities)) try: # exponential fit; curve fitting fails sometimes for unknown reasons (a2, b2, c2), _ = curve_fit(exponential, x, intensities) fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = exponential(x, a2, b2, c2) except: # fall back to linear fit (a1, b1), _ = curve_fit(linear, x, intensities) fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = linear(x, a1, b1) if show: fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, sharey=True) axes = axes.ravel() for side, ax in zip(border_pixel_intensities.keys(), axes): ax.plot(border_pixel_intensities[side]) ax.plot(fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side], ls="--") ax.set_title(side) ax.set_xlabel("Row/Column Index") ax.set_ylabel("Pixel intensity") fig.tight_layout() # Interpolate across the image to compute a correction at every pixel. fitted_frame = np.zeros_like(image) fitted_frame[:, 0] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Left"] fitted_frame[:, -1] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Right"] fitted_frame[ 0, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Top"] fitted_frame[-1, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Bottom"] rows, columns = np.where(fitted_frame) image_correction = griddata( points = np.c_[rows, columns], values = fitted_frame[rows, columns], xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))), method="linear", ) image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape) # Fill in NaNs if there are any in the interpolated image. if np.any(np.isnan(image_correction)) & fill_nans: rows, columns = np.where(~np.isnan(image_correction)) image_correction = griddata( points = np.c_[rows, columns], values = image_correction[rows, columns], xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))), method="nearest", ) image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape) # Apply correction corrected_image = image - image_correction if show: fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, sharex=True, sharey=True, figsize=(15,5)) for img, label, ax in zip( [np.log1p(image), image_correction, np.log1p(corrected_image)], ["Original", "Correction", "Corrected"], axes): ax.imshow(img, cmap="gray") ax.axis("off") ax.set_title(label) return corrected_image if __name__ == "__main__": image = imread("/path/to/test/image.tif") corrected = correct_uneven_illumination(image, 50, show=True, fill_nans=True) plt.show() ``` **Edit:** Made small changes to the code to improve its readability and to improve the output figures. ### Error message ```shell None ``` ### SciPy/NumPy/Python version a simpler example: ```python import numpy as np from scipy.interpolate import griddata import matplotlib.pyplot as plt coords = np.block([[np.arange(300), np.zeros(450)], [np.zeros(300), np.arange(1, 451)]]) values = np.random.rand(750) grid = griddata(coords.T, values, tuple(np.mgrid[:300, :300])) plt.imshow(grid) plt.pause(1) ``` Seven nans, four near the top on the diagonal starting from around x=150, another three near the top on the diagonal from around x=275. Perhaps there should be a statement in the docs that there shouldn't be areas with more than 150 regularly-spaced grid points on two sides and nothing in the interior (or, for that matter, on (or near) the other two sides)? It might be an interaction between the grid spacing of the border points and the grid spacing of the requested grid; changing the spacing of the requested grid to 0.9 rather than one gets rid of most of the nans, though there's still some along the top border and near the bottom point. I don't know that the interpolation was designed for these oblique triangles; the documentation suggests acute triangles were the main design focus, so perhaps someone who knows the code could check it for assumptions? ```pycon >>> coords = np.block([[0, np.arange(200, 300), np.zeros(300 - 200)], [0, np.zeros(300-200), np.arange(200, 300)]]) >>> values = np.random.rand(coords.shape[1]) >>> grid = griddata(coords.T, values, tuple(np.mgrid[:300, :300])) >>> plt.cla(); plt.imshow(grid); plt.pause(1) ``` There's actually more nans if we skip the bits it knows how to do, which is weird. Not entirely sure why it isn't symmetric around `i == j` either.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2492833994/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue. Hi, thanks for taking the time to look at this bug report. # Short summary `scipy.interpolate.griddata` is returning NaNs with `method = 'linear'` and `method = 'cubic'`. By construction, the data points used in the interpolation correspond to the convex hull of the data. Also, there are no duplicate data points. Potentially related to issue #12945. # Detailed description I am trying to correct for uneven illumination in a series of microscopy images. The region of interest in these images is always in the center, such that the border of the images only contains background illumination. This allows me to extract the pixel intensities on the border of the image: ![Figure_1](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5fdebde5-9371-404e-9731-ae636475b85a) I then compute smooth estimates of the pixel intensities for the left/right/top/bottom borders. ![Figure_4](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fd1765ad-dd35-4c80-b636-bfe00305fb24) I can then interpolate between the fitted border pixel intensities using `scipy.interpolate.griddata`. However, the result contains a number of NaNs (run code below with `fill_nans=False`). ![Figure_5](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e021eeee-d597-4468-b50b-55275c1178c7) I can fill in these NaNs by simply running the interpolation again (run code below with `fill_nans=True`) , however as the images are large, this is a slow process that I would like to avoid. ![Figure_3](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ba5dbea0-3929-4c26-99a0-c735279c6234) **Any insight why the output of the first interpolation contains NaN would be appreciated.** [Test image](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ykZP1voNEMKdC79A3_JMjs1DbByJK8wK/view?usp=drive_link) ### Reproducing Code Example ```python import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from skimage.io import imread from scipy.optimize import curve_fit from scipy.interpolate import griddata def correct_uneven_illumination(image, border_width=50, show=False, fill_nans=True): # Extract border pixel intensities border_pixel_intensities = dict( Left = image[:, :border_width].mean(axis=1), Right = image[:, -border_width:].mean(axis=1), Top = image[:border_width, :].mean(axis=0), Bottom = image[-border_width:, :].mean(axis=0) ) if show: image_with_border = image.copy() image_max = image.max() image_with_border[:, border_width] = 2 * image_max image_with_border[:, -border_width] = 2 * image_max image_with_border[ border_width, :] = 2 * image_max image_with_border[-border_width, :] = 2 * image_max fig, ax = plt.subplots() ax.imshow(np.log1p(image_with_border), cmap="gray") # Get a smooth estimate of the variation in illumination along the borders of the image. def linear(x, a, b): return a * x + b def exponential(x, a, b, c): return a * np.exp(-b * x) + c fitted_border_pixel_intensities = dict() for side, intensities in border_pixel_intensities.items(): x = np.arange(len(intensities)) try: # exponential fit; curve fitting fails sometimes for unknown reasons (a2, b2, c2), _ = curve_fit(exponential, x, intensities) fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = exponential(x, a2, b2, c2) except: # fall back to linear fit (a1, b1), _ = curve_fit(linear, x, intensities) fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side] = linear(x, a1, b1) if show: fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, sharey=True) axes = axes.ravel() for side, ax in zip(border_pixel_intensities.keys(), axes): ax.plot(border_pixel_intensities[side]) ax.plot(fitted_border_pixel_intensities[side], ls="--") ax.set_title(side) ax.set_xlabel("Row/Column Index") ax.set_ylabel("Pixel intensity") fig.tight_layout() # Interpolate across the image to compute a correction at every pixel. fitted_frame = np.zeros_like(image) fitted_frame[:, 0] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Left"] fitted_frame[:, -1] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Right"] fitted_frame[ 0, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Top"] fitted_frame[-1, :] = fitted_border_pixel_intensities["Bottom"] rows, columns = np.where(fitted_frame) image_correction = griddata( points = np.c_[rows, columns], values = fitted_frame[rows, columns], xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))), method="linear", ) image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape) # Fill in NaNs if there are any in the interpolated image. if np.any(np.isnan(image_correction)) & fill_nans: rows, columns = np.where(~np.isnan(image_correction)) image_correction = griddata( points = np.c_[rows, columns], values = image_correction[rows, columns], xi = np.transpose(np.where(np.ones_like(image))), method="nearest", ) image_correction = image_correction.reshape(image.shape) # Apply correction corrected_image = image - image_correction if show: fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, sharex=True, sharey=True, figsize=(15,5)) for img, label, ax in zip( [np.log1p(image), image_correction, np.log1p(corrected_image)], ["Original", "Correction", "Corrected"], axes): ax.imshow(img, cmap="gray") ax.axis("off") ax.set_title(label) return corrected_image if __name__ == "__main__": image = imread("/path/to/test/image.tif") corrected = correct_uneven_illumination(image, 50, show=True, fill_nans=True) plt.show() ``` **Edit:** Made small changes to the code to improve its readability and to improve the output figures. ### Error message ```shell None ``` ### SciPy/NumPy/Python version {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27476","id":2556364512,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86YXwbg","number":27476,"title":"ENH: Add a Function to Calculate Sum of Squares of an Array","user":{"login":"RoyiAvital","id":7347975,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDc5NzU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7347975?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital","html_url":"https://github.com/RoyiAvital","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-09-30T11:28:26Z","updated_at":"2024-10-02T22:19:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

The need to calculate for a given vector its _sum of squares_ is common in _Scientific Programming_.

If the vector is materialized an easy option is using the `np.inner()` option with itself.
It is as quick as it gets with, probably, no memory allocation.

Yet if there is a vector to materialize within an expression there are only 2 options:
1. Use `np.linalg.norm()` and square the answer.
2. Use `np.sum(np.square())` which creates an intermediate allocation.

It would be great to have `np.sum_squares()` and `np.fsum_squares()` to sum the square of each element (Where `fsum_squares()` is specialized for the non complex case).
The requirement is to be fast with no allocation.  
For instance, have this use case optimized (Ridge Regression $f \\left( \\boldsymbol{x}, \\lambda \\right) = \\frac{1}{2} {\\left\\| \\boldsymbol{A} \\boldsymbol{x} - \\boldsymbol{b} \\right\\|}_{2}^{2} + \\frac{\\lambda}{2} \\left\\| \\boldsymbol{x} \\right\\|\_{2}^{2}$):

```python
hObjFun = lambda vX, λ: 0.5 * np.fsum_squares(mA @ vX - vB) + (λ / 2.0) * np.fsum_squares(vX)
```

Alternatives:
 - Add an option in `np.linalg.norm()` to skip the last calculation in `sum(abs(x)**ord)**(1./ord)`. Namely not applying the `1./ord` operation. This is a generalization of the above for $\\left\\| \\boldsymbol{x} \\right\\|_{p}^{p}$.
 - Add support for a single input case for `np.inner()` which will apply the operation of the array with itself.

At any case it seems they could share most of the code.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2389786717","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27476#issuecomment-2389786717","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476","id":2389786717,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86OcUBd","user":{"login":"DWesl","id":22566757,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNTY2NzU3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22566757?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl","html_url":"https://github.com/DWesl","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-02T22:19:32Z","updated_at":"2024-10-02T22:19:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Your suggestion is to create a helper function
```python
def sum_squares(x):
    return np.inner(x, x)
```
in NumPy, rather than your own code?

(or
```python
def sum_square(x):
    result = la.norm(x)
    result **= 2
    return result
```
for your alternative suggestion, each with about a 20-line docstring if done properly)

What advantage does including this function in NumPy have over adding it as an example in the documentation somewhere?

Alternately, if you prefer every program be a one-liner, the following should work:
```python
optimize(lambda vX, weight: 0.5 * (lambda x: np.inner(x, x))(mA @ vX - vB) + 0.5 * weight * (lambda x: np.inner(x, x))(vX), ...)
```

https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/cd4d6ba5d1aee621abb9d7d52ecb48cb7bc43c98/numpy/linalg/_linalg.py#L2743-L2744
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/cd4d6ba5d1aee621abb9d7d52ecb48cb7bc43c98/numpy/linalg/_linalg.py#L2786-L2790
your helper function will not be slower than the original, especially if you enforce the use of a static type checker to avoid the runtime checks NumPy has earlier in that function (the first helper function is basically line 2743, without the argument regularization and case selection logic earlier in the function).

There was a big fuss some years back about Python adding an assignment operator:
```python
def hObjFun(vX, weight):
    return 0.5 * ((residuals := mA @ vX - vB).dot(residuals) + weight * vX.dot(vX))
```
which solves the "array doesn't have a name I can use in two places" problem in a way that doesn't produce a second line in the function, though you would still have to come up with a name for the expression, so if that's the bottleneck you're facing, this doesn't help.

Alternately:
```python
def norm_ne(vector, p=2):
    return numexpr.evaluate(f"sum(abs(vector) ** {p:d})", {"vector": vector})

@numba.njit
def norm_nm(vector, p=2):
    return np.sum(np.abs(vector) ** p)
```
should reduce function call overhead (especially on repeated calls), or you could write Cython, Pythran, Fortran/f2py, or C++/pybind11 versions where any functions called are C/Fortran not Python and therefore have smaller overhead.  (Depending on the requirements of `numexpr.re_evaluate`, you might be able to speed things further in whichever loop).

Scipy Team,

Is it possible to expose the low-level special functions from scipy.special.cython_special as C symbols in a shared library (e.g., libscipy_special.so), similar to how libraries like GSL (libgsl.so) and LAPACK (libopenblas.so) expose their functions?
The goal is to enable low-level access to SciPy’s special functions directly within Numba-compiled functions and being able to cache them just by using Python code.

Here is an example how it suppose to work (using GSL instead of SciPy):
```
from numba import njit
from numba.core import types, typing
from llvmlite.binding import load_library_permanently
from ctypes.util import find_library

# Load the GSL shared library
# make sure that GSL is installed: sudo apt install libgsl-dev
gsl_library_path = find_library('gsl')
load_library_permanently(gsl_library_path)

# Define the GSL Bessel function signature
c_func_name = 'gsl_sf_bessel_J0'
return_type = types.float64
arg_type = types.float64
c_sig = typing.signature(return_type, arg_type)
c_func = types.ExternalFunction(c_func_name, c_sig)

@njit  # (cache=True)  # jitted function is cachable
def compute_gsl_func(x):
    return c_func(x)

# Test value
print(f"GSL result: {compute_gsl_func(5.0)}")
# GSL result: -0.17759677131433826
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21681/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21681/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2418034617","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/21681#issuecomment-2418034617","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21681","id":2418034617,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6QIEe5","user":{"login":"DWesl","id":22566757,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNTY2NzU3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22566757?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl","html_url":"https://github.com/DWesl","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-16T21:55:09Z","updated_at":"2024-10-16T21:55:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Does the `numba.extending.get_cython_function_address` function mentioned [here](https://numba.readthedocs.io/en/stable/extending/high-level.html#importing-cython-functions) not work?  I can't tell if it needs that `api` keyword, nor whether inclusion in a `pxd` file counts for that purpose.

For your question about how to tell Numba to use C++ functions from a shared library `dlopen`ed in the current process, I suspect the Numba maintainers would have a better idea, but I think the approach you outlined will run into issues with C++ name mangling.

As an alternative, when checking whether Numba already had functions to ease the use of C++ functions, I found [CFFI was among the suggsetions](https://numba.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user/5minguide.html#other-things-of-interest) for similar purposes.  CFFI can work the way you want (i.e. like `ctypes`), but it turns out it does not support C++, [suggesting instead](https://cffi.readthedocs.io/en/stable/goals.html) `cppyy`, [which has a Numba plugin](https://cppyy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/numba.html), so perhaps you might look there as well.

_No response_","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27554/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27554/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2430463145","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27554#issuecomment-2430463145","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27554","id":2430463145,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Q3eyp","user":{"login":"DWesl","id":22566757,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyNTY2NzU3","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22566757?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl","html_url":"https://github.com/DWesl","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DWesl/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-22T22:55:07Z","updated_at":"2024-10-22T22:55:07Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"SymPy has [an implementation of Hermite normal form](https://docs.sympy.org/latest/modules/matrices/normalforms.html#sympy.matrices.normalforms.hermite_normal_form), if you want to try that.

For more fun, the determinant of the transpose of that matrix is exactly zero, the LU factorization of the original matrix produces an equivalent estimate, and the QR and SVD factorizations produce determinant estimates slightly smaller than the others:
```pycon
>>> import numpy as np
>>> import numpy.linalg as la
>>> from scipy import linalg
>>> arr = np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]])
>>> la.det(arr)
-3.1974423109204565e-14
>>> la.det(arr.T)
0.0
>>> np.prod(la.svd(arr)[1])
9.990753062948142e-15
>>> np.prod(np.diag(la.qr(arr)[1]))
-9.243157666806292e-15
>>> la.slogdet(arr)
SlogdetResult(sign=-1.0, logabsdet=-31.073840089541154)
>>> np.exp(-31.07384)
3.197442597223141e-14
>>> np.prod(np.diag(linalg.lu(arr)[2]))
3.197442310920451e-14
>>> linalg.lu(arr)[0]
array([[0., 1., 0.],
       [1., 0., 0.],
       [0., 0., 1.]])
```
All of these estimates of the determinant are less than the largest element of the matrix times the size of the matrix times the resolution of the type the calculations are done in (`abs(la.det(arr)) < 1e-15 * arr.size * arr.max()`), so I would be inclined to count them as equivalent to zero.  Given the existence of the Hermite Normal Form, it is possible that the threshold could be *much* larger, perhaps a flat 1e-6 or even 1e-3.

[A comment on](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27599#issuecomment-2425430019) #27599 suggests [`la.matrix_rank`](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.linalg.matrix_rank.html) for determining whether a given matrix is singular; it basically combines the suggestions and rules-of-thumb above.  The actual test for singularity would then be `la.matrix_rank(arr) < min(arr.shape)`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2430463145/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Using Numpy 2.x (tried 2.02, 2.1.2) I can no longer access module data.  

After `f2py` I get the `pyf` file
```fortran
!    -*- f90 -*-
! Note: the context of this file is case sensitive.

python module _solver ! in 
    interface  ! in :_solver
        module types ! in :_solver:solver.f90

but when I try to access the data, as suggested in the online doc (just checking that this is still as it used to be, https://numpy.org/doc/2.1/f2py/python-usage.html#fortran-90-module-data), I get

so, not trace of `module lanedata`.  This used to work (and still does) in Numpy 1.x.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
from laneemden._solver import lanedata
```

Here my code, `solver.f90`

```
! 20111212 Alexander Heger

! 1/z**2 d/dz (z**2 d/dz theta(z)) + theta(z)**n = 0
! for small z one can approximate
! theta(z) = 1. + (-1/6.)*z**2 + (n/120.)*z**4 + O(z**6)
! Therefore lim(z)-->0 d**2 theta(z)/d z**2 = -1/3

! 20120423 Alexander Heger

! if we include a constant rotation rate Omega the equation becomes
! 1/z**2 d/dz (z**2 d/dz theta(z)) + theta(z)**n - w = 0
! where
! w = W/rho_c
! W = 2 Omega**2 / 4 pi G
! for small z one can approximate
! theta(z) = 1. + (w - 1)/6. *z**2 + (1.-w)*(n/120.)*z**4 + O(z**6)
! Therefore lim(z)-->0 d**2 theta(z)/d z**2 = (w-1.)/3

module types

  implicit none

  INTEGER, PARAMETER :: int32 = SELECTED_INT_KIND(8)
  INTEGER, PARAMETER :: int64 = SELECTED_INT_KIND(16)
  INTEGER, PARAMETER :: real64 = SELECTED_REAL_KIND(15)

end module types


module lanedata

  use types, only: &
       real64, int32

  implicit none

  save

  integer(kind=int32), parameter :: &
       maxdata = 2**20-1

  real(kind=real64), dimension(:,:), allocatable :: &
       theta

  integer(kind=int32) :: &
       ndata

end module lanedata


subroutine freelanedata()

  use lanedata, only: &
       ndata, theta

  implicit none

  if (allocated(theta)) deallocate(theta)
  ndata = -1

end subroutine freelanedata


subroutine lane(dx, n, w)

  ! lane emden integration, return data,
  ! including first invalid (rho < 0) point for interpolation.

  use types, only: &
       int32, real64

  use lanedata, only: &
       maxdata, theta, ndata

  implicit none

!f2py real(kind=real64), intent(in) :: dx, n, w

  real(kind=real64), intent(in) :: &
       dx, n, w

  real(kind=real64) :: &
       x
  integer(kind=int32) :: &
       i, j

  real(kind=real64), dimension(0:1) :: &
       y, z

  real(kind=real64), dimension(:,:), allocatable :: &
       theta_

  if (allocated(theta)) deallocate(theta)
  j = maxdata
  allocate(theta_(0:j, 0:1))

  x = 0.d0
  y(0:1) = (/1.d0, 0.d0/)

  i = 0
  theta_(i,:) = y(:)
  do while (.True.)
     call rk4(x,y(0),y(1),dx,n,w,z(0),z(1))
     if (i == j) then
        j = j + maxdata
        allocate(theta(0:j,0:1))
        theta(0:i,:) = theta_(0:i,:)
        deallocate(theta_)
        call move_alloc(theta, theta_)
     endif

     i = i+1
     theta_(i, :) = z(:)
     x = x + dx
     y(:) = z(:)

     if (y(0) < 0.d0) exit
  enddo
  ndata = i

  allocate(theta(0:ndata, 0:1))
  theta(0:ndata,0:1) = theta_(0:ndata,0:1)
  deallocate(theta_)

end subroutine lane


subroutine rk4(x0, y0, y1, dx, n, w, z0, z1)

  use types, only: &
       real64

  implicit none

  real(kind=real64), parameter :: &
       p13 = 1.d0 / 3.d0, &
       p16 = 1.d0 / 6.d0

  real(kind=real64), intent(in)  :: &
       x0, y0, y1
  real(kind=real64), intent(in)  :: &
       dx, n, w
  real(kind=real64), intent(out) :: &
       z0, z1

  real(kind=real64) :: &
       xh, dh
  real(kind=real64) :: &
       k10, k11, k20, k21, k30, k31, k40, k41

!f2py real(8), intent(in) :: x0, dx, n, w
!f2py real(8), intent(in) :: y0, y1
!f2py real(8), intent(out) :: z0, z1

  xh = x0 + 0.5d0 * dx
  dh = 0.5d0 * dx

  k10 = y1
  if (x0 == 0) then
     k11 = (w - 1.d0) * p13
  else
     k11 = -2.d0 / x0 * y1 - (max(y0, 0.d0))**n + w
  endif

  k20 = y1 + dh*k11
  k21 = -2.d0 / xh * k20 - (max(y0 + dh*k10, 0.d0))**n

  k30 = y1 + dh*k21
  k31 = -2.d0 / xh * k30 - (max(y0 + dh*k20, 0.d0))**n

  k40 = y1 + dx*k31
  k41 = -2.d0 / (x0+dx) * k40 - (max(y0 + dx*k30,0.d0))**n

  z0 = y0 + dx*(k10 + 2.d0 * (k20 + k30) + k40) * p16
  z1 = y1 + dx*(k11 + 2.d0 * (k21 + k31) + k41) * p16

end subroutine rk4
```
I use a custom build package that does not work with meson (and sadly, the usually very helpful Numpy developers out of principle refused to include fixes to obvious bugs in the build script to this day and a small patch to make my script continue to work, for both of which I had posted patches) and that are lengthy, so I won't include.

The issue seems to be that there is on interface in module lanedata.  If I include an interface into the definition of `module lanedata` so it becomes, e.g., 
```fortran
module lanedata

  use types, only: &
       real64, int32

  implicit none

  save

  integer(kind=int32), parameter :: &
       maxdata = 2**20-1

  real(kind=real64), dimension(:,:), allocatable :: &
       theta

  integer(kind=int32) :: &
       ndata

contains

  subroutine phoney
  end subroutine phoney
  
end module lanedata
```
then the module is included and an interface generated.  This behaviour to skip modules w/o interface contents (just adding the `contains` line but an empty section does not suffice).  

Maybe the Python 1.x behaviour can be restored, as it is common to have plain data modules w/o definition of functions or subroutines.

It is also possible that I missed a flag / behaviour change, my apologies in this case and please advise.
